title: Velocity-Controlled Directional Readout of Single Photons
abstract: Photodetection is usually treated in the frame in which the detector is at rest relative to the optical apparatus. We show that uniform motion of an electric Glauber detector changes the single-click POVM realized on two counterpropagating single-photon modes. Motion Doppler-shifts the alternatives in the detector frame; finite bandwidth then converts propagation direction into a detection bias without decohering the photon. For a Lorentzian response near one Doppler branch, the readout crosses from phase-sensitive to direction-sensitive with a quality-factor-enhanced onset. Finite-time integration adds Doppler-beat visibility loss, separating passive covariance from measurement change.
summary: This paper shows that moving a detector relative to a photon can change the type of information extracted from a single-photon state. By using a narrowband detector, one can use the Doppler shift to switch from measuring interference to measuring propagation direction. This effect is operational and does not require decohering the photon itself.
main_problem: Investigating how the uniform motion of a detector relative to an optical apparatus changes the measurement-theoretic properties (POVM) of single-photon detection, specifically the transition from phase-sensitive to direction-sensitive readout.
main_result: The paper demonstrates that detector motion, combined with finite spectral bandwidth, can transform a phase-sensitive measurement into a direction-sensitive one without decohering the photon, with the transition being enhanced by the detector's quality factor.
method: The author uses the Glauber formulation of photodetection, Lorentz transformations of electromagnetic fields, and the theory of Positive-Operator-Valued Measures (POVM) to derive the single-click effect density.
model_or_system: A single-photon state in two counterpropagating modes (+ and -) interacting with an electric Glauber detector moving at a constant velocity v along the x-axis, modeled with a Lorentzian spectral response.
key_observables: Instantaneous Fringe Visibility (V_beta), Directional Bias (B_beta), and Doppler-beat frequency (Delta_Omega).
important_parameters: Normalized velocity (beta), detector Quality Factor (Q), relative phase (phi), and the integration time window (T).
main_assumptions: The detector is treated as having a Lorentzian susceptibility; the motion is assumed to be uniform; the analysis considers both broadband and narrowband limits.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the geometry of a detector moving along the x-axis probing two counterpropagating modes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of detector motion, establishes the Lorentz-transformed field model, derives the POVM for the moving detector, analyzes the broadband and narrowband (Q-enhanced) regimes, and discusses the effects of finite-time integration.
why_interesting: It provides a novel way to control measurement axes on a propagation qubit using kinematic motion and spectral selectivity, which is highly relevant for engineered quantum platforms like cQED and optomechanics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Moving-detector geometry. A detector moving along +x with velocity v = βc probes two counterpropagating single-photon modes of the same laboratory frequency ω. The right-propagating blue mode is annihilated by ˆa+ and carries wave vector +k, while the left-propagating red mode is annihilated by ˆa−and carries wave vector −k. Although the detector is purely electric in its rest frame, its laboratory-frame response mixes electric and magnetic ampli- tudes and samples the two modes at the Doppler-shifted frequencies Ω± = γ(1 ∓β)ω. This is the origin of the velocity-dependent pho- todetection POVM derived below.

Figure 2. Moving-detector photodetection and Doppler- enhanced directional readout. (a) Broadband limit. A detec- tor that is purely electric in its rest frame realizes, in the labora- tory frame, a velocity-fixed electric–magnetic readout. The visi- bility Vbroad decreases as the propagation bias |Bbroad| increases, while the ideal click effect satisfies V2 + B2 = 1. (b) Narrowband mechanism. A Lorentzian detector tuned near one Doppler-shifted component samples the two counterpropagating alternatives at dif- ferent detector-frame frequencies, Ω+ and Ω−. Propagation selec- tivity appears once the Doppler splitting resolves the linewidth. (c) Q-enhanced crossover. For a Lorentzian detector...

Figure 3. Observed visibility in the presence of spectral selec- tivity and finite-time integration. The color map shows the visi- bility Vobs as a function of two dimensionless control parameters: the spectral-selectivity parameter βQ, proportional to the Doppler splitting in linewidth units, and the time-averaging parameter βωT, which measures the accumulated Doppler-beat phase during the de- tection window. The vertical dashed line marks the narrowband onset βQ ≃1/4 for a branch-tuned Lorentzian response, where the detec- tor begins to resolve the two propagation alternatives spectrally.
